summary: An excerpt from Walter Isaacson's biography detailing Steve Jobs's early life, including his adoption, his relationship with his parents, and his formative exposures to Silicon Valley, electronics, and design. It highlights his early beliefs on craftsmanship, hardware-software integration, and his rebellious attitude toward traditional authority.

principles.statement: Care about the look of the parts you can't see.
principles.rationale: True craftsmanship requires doing things right completely, which means paying attention to the hidden elements of a product just as much as the visible ones.
principles.source_quote: He loved doing things right. He even cared about the look of the parts you couldn’t see.

principles.statement: Bring great design and simple capability to the mass market.
principles.rationale: Clean, elegant design shouldn't be a luxury; it should be accessible and affordable for everyday people.
principles.source_quote: I love it when you can bring really great design and simple capability to something that doesn’t cost much.

principles.statement: Hardware and software should be engineered to work together instantly.
principles.rationale: Technology should function seamlessly out of the box, hardwired to work as a single integrated system rather than requiring the user to piece it together.
principles.source_quote: It was as if something in the animal’s body and in its brain had been engineered to work together instantly rather than being learned.

principles.statement: True spirituality is about lived experience, not received dogma.
principles.rationale: Religion loses its value when it relies purely on blind faith rather than actively living out the teachings and seeing the world through that lens.
principles.source_quote: The juice goes out of Christianity when it becomes too based on faith rather than on living like Jesus or seeing the world as Jesus saw it.

mental_models.name: The Newborn Calf (Integrated Systems)
mental_models.description: Viewing a technological product as a biological organism where the 'body' (hardware) and 'brain' (software) are hardwired to work together instantly upon birth, rather than being learned or assembled after the fact.
mental_models.example: Jobs used the metaphor of a calf walking minutes after being born to describe how Apple's hardware and software should be engineered to work together instantly.

heuristics: You can build and understand anything if you take it apart and learn the theory behind it.

signature_quotes.text: I love it when you can bring really great design and simple capability to something that doesn’t cost much.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the Eichler homes in his childhood neighborhood and how they inspired the original vision for Apple.

signature_quotes.text: Knowing I was adopted may have made me feel more independent, but I have never felt abandoned. I’ve always felt special. My parents made me feel special.
signature_quotes.context: Dismissing the psychological theory that his drive came from the trauma of being abandoned at birth.

signature_quotes.text: They were my sperm and egg bank. That’s not harsh, it’s just the way it was, a sperm bank thing, nothing more.
The juice goes out of Christianity when it becomes too based on faith rather than on living like Jesus or seeing the world as Jesus saw it.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining his views on religion and why he left the church as a teenager.

anti_patterns.description: Rote memorization and rigid authority in education.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It fails to stimulate intelligent children and instead comes close to 'beating any curiosity' out of them.

anti_patterns.description: Sucking up to people to make a sale.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It requires adopting a servile attitude that compromises a person's natural dignity and integrity.
